text: The surgeon begins a total laparoscopic hysterectomy by cutting the round ligament, fallopian tube, and utero-ovarian ligament with a harmonic ace to conserve both adnexa. A bipolar device is then used to control the adnexal vein and artery, cutting the pedicle one centimeter outside the uterine cornea. The dissection continues with the harmonic ace, freeing the adnexa from its uterine attachment.

The surgeon uses gas to assist in dissecting and opening both aspects of the broad ligament, moving towards the bladder. Due to the patient's previous C-section scars, finding the correct plane is challenging. The posterior leaf of the broad ligament is cut to skeletonize the uterine pedicle, enhancing control over the right uterine artery and vein.

The surgeon applies bipolar energy directly to the uterine artery and vein, taking care to avoid damaging the bladder due to previous scar sections. The vesicovaginal peritoneum is carefully removed, and the bladder is retracted cranially. The anterior valve of the uterine manipulator is visible. The round ligament is cut using the harmonic device, and the large adnexal vein is controlled. The tube is controlled with the harmonic, while the utero-ovarian pedicle is managed with bipolar energy for safe hemostasis.

The surgeon uses bipolar energy and harmonic ace to achieve a bloodless section, removing the left adnexa from its corneal attachment. The harmonic ace is positioned in the suprapubic port to remove peritoneum surrounding the uterine artery and vein. After skeletonizing the left uterine pedicle, the surgeon controls and cuts the pedicle using bipolar energy. The procedure is repeated on the opposite side, coagulating and cutting the pedicle to minimize bleeding. The uterine artery and vein are controlled, and the upper part of the uterosacral ligament is cut to increase uterine mobility. A colpotomy is performed using a monopolar hook.

The surgeon uses a monopolar needle to perform a bloodless section, following the uterine manipulator. The upper part of the right uterosacral ligament and the posterior aspect of the vagina are cut. A Chardonnay knife, a cold blade passed through a ten-millimeter trocar, is used for uterine hemissection, dividing the uterus into two equal pieces. Care is taken to avoid bowel damage during this procedure, and the uterus is removed via vaginal traction.

The surgeon begins the vaginal closure using a large 64 needle with a V-cremant stitch, preferring interrupted sutures over running ones. The closure starts at the left and right edges, incorporating the upper part of the uterosacral ligament into the stitch to reduce the risk of postoperative vaginal prolapse. The median part of the vagina is closed using the same technique, with sutures placed from the anterior to the posterior aspect, employing an intracorporeal knotting technique for secure closure.
The surgeon uses interrupted sutures to close the vaginal cuff, ensuring complete closure to reduce the risk of ischemia. The procedure conserves both the fallopian tubes and ovaries.
